## - FINITE-TIME CONVERGENCE AND SAMPLE COM - PLEXITY OF MULTI-AGENT ACTOR-CRITIC REIN FORCEMENT LEARNING WITH AVERAGE REWARD
Hairi and Jia Liu Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering The Ohio State University Columbus, OH 43210, USA hairi.1@osu.edu, liu@ece.osu.edu
Songtao Lu IBM Research AI IBM Thomas J. Watson Research Center Yorktown Heights, NY 10598 songtao@ibm.com

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we establish the first finite-time convergence result of the actor-critic algorithm for fully decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) problems with average reward. In this problem, a set of N agents work cooperatively to maximize the global average reward through interacting with their neighbors over a communication network. We consider a practical MARL setting, where the rewards and actions of each agent are only known to itself, and the knowledge of joint actions of the agents is not assumed. Toward this end, we propose a mini-batch Markovian sampled fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm and analyze its finite-time convergence and sample complexity. We show that the sample complexity of this algorithm is O(N[2] /ǫ[2] log(N/ǫ)). Interestingly, this sample complexity bound matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent actorcritic algorithms for reinforcement learning.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

1) Background and Motivations: In recent years, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has found a wide range of applications in networked large-scale systems, such as power grid systems (Riedmiller et al., 2000), autonomous driving (Yu et al., 2019; Shalev-Shwartz et al., 2016) and strategic games (Silver et al., 2018; Foerster et al., 2018), to name just a few. Although empirical successes of MARL applications have been widely observed, the fundamental theoretical understanding of how to develop fast-converging and low sample-complexity MARL algorithms, two of the most important performance metrics for MARL, remains in its infancy so far (see, e.g., (Zhang et al., 2021a) for an excellent survey). In particular, two important aspects of cooperative MARL algorithm designs deserve special attention:

- First, in the multi-agent collaborative setting, the information structure (i.e., the assumptions of who have the knowledge of what) is far more complex than its single-agent counterpart and care must be taken in MARL problem formulations. In the cooperative MARL literature so far, many existing works assume full knowledge of joint states and joint actions, which often do not hold true in practice. For example, in autonomous driving (Yu et al., 2019), each vehicle can only observe/detect the actions of the surrounding vehicles that are within its communication range. As another example, in power grid networks (Riedmiller et al., 2000), each power distributor generally does not know the resistor values set by other distributors.

- Second, most MARL theoretical studies in the MARL literature are focused on the discounted total reward setting, where a hyperparameter γ ∈ (0, 1) is introduced as the discount factor in the objective function. Although the discounted total reward setting captures the important aspect of diminishing return in the future, it may not be appropriate for many other applications where the long-term average reward is of interest. For example, in the optimization of distributed communication networks with MARL, the typical and natural performance metrics are long-term average throughput or latency in the steady-state.
The lack of a fundamental understanding on how to develop efficient cooperative MARL algorithms that consider the above two aspects in terms of information structure, scalability, and communication

and sample complexities motivates us to fill this gap by developing a fully decentralized cooperative MARL algorithm in the average reward setting, without assuming joint action knowledge.

2) Technical Challenges: Developing a fully decentralized cooperative MARL algorithm for the average reward setting without full joint action knowledge is highly non-trivial and several major technical challenges naturally arise. First, it is well-known that, even in the single-agent reinforcement learning (RL) setting, the average reward setting is more challenging to analyze compared to the discounted reward setting, which necessitates different proof techniques (Tsitsiklis & Van Roy, 1999). In MARL, the decentralized nature and the lack of joint action-state information further complicate the algorithm design and analysis in the average reward setting. Second, due to the lack of joint action knowledge, the communication costs among agents in MARL will be significantly increased to achieve a satisfying performance, which implies that low sample and communication complexities are even more challenging and critical for MARL without joint action information.

3) Main Results and Contributions: The main contribution of this paper is that we overcome the aforementioned challenges and develop a consensus-based Markovian sampled decentralized actorcritic algorithm for MARL. Our key results in this paper are summarized as follows:

- We propose a batch-sampled actor-critic algorithm that uses consensus updates in TD-sharing among agents. This batch sampling approach enables more efficient communication compared to the classical fully decentralized MARL. Specifically, in order to converge to an ǫ neighborhood of the stationary, we require O(ǫ[−][1] log(ǫ[−][1] )) rounds of communication while only needing O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) samples. By contrast, the state-of-the-art MARL requires a communication round per sampling. Also, our algorithm allows the use of constant step-sizes in both the actor and the critic steps.

- We provide the first-ever sample complexity analysis in the MARL average reward problem setting without joint action information. Our obtained complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), where ǫ is the closeness to the neighborhood of stationary point (treating network size N as a fixed constant). It is worth noting that the order-wise sample complexity of our algorithm matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent RL algorithms.

## 2 RELATED WORK

In this section, we provide a quick overview on the closely related work on MARL algorithms and their theoretical results, along with several notable related counterparts in single-agent RL.

1) MARL Theoretical Analysis and Algorithm Design: For recent advances in MARL algorithms and their theoretical results, Zhang et al. (2021a) provided a comprehensive survey. Also, Lee et al. (2020) highlighted the evolution from single-agent to multi-agent RL from a distributed optimization perspective. In the broader area of MARL, a line of research has been focused on the MARL policy evaluation problem. These works analyzed the convergence Doan et al. (2019) and proposed various variance reduction of policy evaluation in decentralized MARL algorithms Zhang et al. (2021b). Doan et al. (2019) used i.i.d. sampling and has shown the sample complexity of O[˜] (ǫ[−][1] ) for their TD(0) learning algorithm to reach a mean-square error convergence. However, these algorithms do not involve policy improvement and solely focus on the performance evaluation of given policies. In the areas of joint policy evaluation and improvement, Foerster et al. (2018) considered multi-agent actor-critic algorithm that has a centralized critic and decentralized actors, which is different from our fully decentralized actor-critic algorithm. In contrast, Zhang et al. (2018) established asymptotic convergence results for fully decentralized MARL actor-critic algorithms. Concurrent with our work, Chen et al. (2021) has recently studied the mini-batch Markovian sampling actor-critic algorithm for a class of discounted reward MARL problems, where the finite-time convergence result is obtained. They have applied batch sampling for both actor and critic steps and achieved a sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), which is the same as ours. We note that, together with our work, these are the first finite-time convergence results for MARL. However, there are several key differences between our work and (Chen et al., 2021). First, we focus on the average reward problem, while Chen et al. (2021) studied the discounted reward setting. Second, in (Chen et al., 2021), agents share a noisy version of the rewards with the neighbors, which requires a re-sampling process from every sampled reward instance. In contrast, we allow agents to share local TD-errors with their neighbors and no re-sampling is required.

2) Related Literature in Single-Agent RL: We note that single-agent RL can be viewed as a centralized approach, where a central controller collects joint actions, rewards and even designs policies for agents. For the single-agent average reward setting, Tsitsiklis & Van Roy (1999) first analyzed the asymptotic convergence of TD(λ) algorithm with function approximations in the policy evaluation problem. Also, Tsitsiklis & Van Roy (2002) provided insights in terms of differences and connections between average reward and discounted reward of TD-based learning algorithms with function approximations. Recently, Qiu et al. (2021) analyzed the sample complexity for an actorcritic algorithm for the average reward problem. In their actor-critic algorithm, they used batch sampling for the critic learning and i.i.d. sampling for the actor step, with sample complexity being O(ǫ[−][3] log[2] (ǫ[−][1] )). By applying mini-batch sampling update, we are able to improve the sample complexity by a factor of O(ǫ[−][1] ). Another closely related work on single-agent RL is (Xu et al., 2020), where the authors studied the discounted reward problem. They used batch sampling for both actor and critic steps in their actor-critic algorithm and developed a new technique to handle bias error in the critic step, which we also adopted for the average approximation parameter analysis in our critic step. This achieved the state-of-the-art sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) for singleagent RL. In addition, the global convergence of actor-critic algorithm to the optimal policy has been studied in the case of discounted setting with single time scale in (Fu et al., 2020) and linear quadratic regulator in (Yang et al., 2019). However, we note that these settings are fundamentally different from the average reward setting and it will be an interesting future direction to consider global convergence possibility in the average reward setting.
To conclude this section, we summarize the aforementioned related actor-critic and TD algorithms and their sample complexity results in Table 1.

## 3 MULTI-AGENT REINFORCEMENT LEARNING WITH AVERAGE REWARD

## 3.1 SYSTEM MODEL

Consider a multi-agent system with N agents, denoted by N = {1, · · · , N }, operating in a networked environment. Let E be the edge set for a given network G = (N , E). To formulate our MARL problem and facilitate our subsequent discussions, we first define the notion of networked multi-agent MDP as follows.

Definition 1 (Networked Multi-Agent MDP). Let G = (N , E) be a communication network that connects N agents. A networked multi-agent MDP is defined by following tuple (S, {A[i] }i∈N , P, {R[i] }i∈N , G), where S is the global state space observed by all agents, A[i] is the action set for agent i, and P : S ×A×S → [0, 1] is a global state transition function, and R[i] : S ×A is the local reward function for agent i. Let A =[�] i∈N[A][i][ be the joint action set of all agents.]

In this paper, we assume that the global state space S and action space for agent A[i] are finite. As a result, the joint action space A is also finite for finite N . We also note that at time t ≥ 0, all agents can observe the current global state st. However, agent i can only observe its own action a[i] t[∈A][i][, which is the key difference between our model and that in (Zhang et al., 2018), where it is] assumed that the joint actions are observable to all agents. Moreover, each agent can only observe its own reward rt[i][,][i.e.,][agents][do][not][observe][or][share][rewards][with][other][agents][at][time][t][.][The]

## 4 A CONSENSUS-BASED ACTOR-CRITIC ALGORITHM

In this section, we propose a consensus-based actor-critic algorithm that includes two key steps: actor and critic. In the critic step, the algorithm evaluates the value functions for the policy πθt at time t. After the critic step, the algorithm enters the actor step, which improves the policy parameter θt according to the direction from policy gradient as shown in Theorem 1. In both steps, we use constant step-sizes and adopt batch sampling.

In this paper, we use linear function approximations for the value functions. Specifically, each agent i has a parameter w[i] ∈ R[K] to approximate the global value functions Vθ(s; w[i] ) for each state s ∈S. For linear approximation, we have Vθ(s; w[i] ) = φ(s)[T] w[i] , where φ(s) ∈ R[K] denotes the feature for state s. As a result, the gradient of value function at state s with respect to approximation parameter w[i] is φ(s), i.e. ∇wVθ(s; w[i] ) = φ(s).

1) The Critic Step: The critic step is achieved through its own oracle, which is summarized in Algorithm 1. In the critic step, we allow the agents to communicate the approximation parameters w(s) with their neighbors via the communication network with consensus weight matrix A. For agent i ∈N , the parameter is locally updated by following rules:

## 5 THEORETICAL CONVERGENCE ANALYSIS

In this section, we present the convergence results for both the critic and actor steps in Theorems 2 and 3, respectively. Due to space limitation, we relegate the proofs to the supplementary material.

Theorem 3 concludes the overall sample complexity of our proposed actor-critic algorithm. The sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) matches the state-of-the-art single-agent actor-critic RL by Xu et al. (2020) and the discounted MARL by Chen et al. (2021).

However, our work is still an improvement compared to the classical MARL in Zhang et al. (2018) for the average reward setting. Specifically, Zhang et al. (2018) needed a communication round after each sampling. By contrast, in this paper, we only need a communication round per O(ǫ[−][1] ) sampling. This is thanks to the use of batch sampling in the actor step. Also because of the batch sampling, we are able to use a constant step-size for both actor and critic steps. Here, the overall communication cost is measured by the number of communication rounds rather than the size of bits transmitted over the network. In this section, we conduct experiments to compare our proposed consensus-based TD-sharing MARL algorithm with the most related MARL algorithm 1 in Zhang et al. (2018) that also studied average reward. To our knowledge, this is the only work that is directly comparable to ours. The key difference is that the knowledge of joint action is assumed in Zhang et al. (2018), but not in our work. We vary the system size from N = 6, 10 to 15. The blue curve is our TD-sharing algorithm and the red curve is classical MARL algorithm in Zhang et al. (2018). The curves represent the average results of 10 trials and the 95% confidence intervals are also plotted. For the details, see A.1 in Appendix. The results in Figure 1 show that for different system sizes, both algorithms converge to a reasonable objective value. Note that if we use uniformly random policy as the baseline policy, then the objective values will be around 2 due to the setting of our experiments. All simulation results are above this threshold and our TD-sharing algorithm converge to a better objective value. See Section A.2 for addition experiment results.

## 7 CONCLUSION AND DISCUSSION

In this paper, we studied fully decentralized MARL in average reward setting and proposed a batch-sampled actor-critic algorithm. Our main contribution is to establish the first finite time convergence result for fully decentralized MARL in average reward setting, where the complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), which matches that of the state-of-the-art single agent RL. The algorithm reaches such convergence with a better communication efficiency. However, it is still in the preliminary stage of the convergence analysis of the MARL since we only used the vanilla average. The future direction will be how to design a more scalable algorithm in terms of system size.

## High-Level Feedback

This paper addresses a well-motivated and timely problem: providing the first finite-time convergence and sample complexity analysis for fully decentralized multi-agent actor-critic reinforcement learning in the *average reward* setting without joint action knowledge. The contribution is clearly positioned against the literature, with the authors carefully distinguishing their work from closely related efforts (e.g., Zhang et al. 2018, which gives only asymptotic results, and Chen et al. 2021, which addresses the discounted case). The headline result—a sample complexity of O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)) that matches single-agent actor-critic rates—is a meaningful theoretical advance, and the emphasis on decoupling communication rounds from sample complexity via mini-batch sampling is a genuinely useful algorithmic insight. The introduction is generally well-written, with a clear motivation-challenges-contributions structure, though clarity suffers in several places: the abstract and contributions section state the complexity results somewhat inconsistently (the N-dependence appears only in the abstract), and the phrase "closeness to the neighborhood of stationary point" is imprecise—readers will need more explicit articulation of what stationarity means here, given the nonconvex actor objective and the function approximation setting. The most significant concerns that a reviewer should probe in the technical sections are: (i) whether the assumptions required for the average-reward analysis (e.g., ergodicity, mixing, bounded features, linear function approximation) are realistic and comparable to those in prior work; (ii) whether the claimed matching of single-agent sample complexity obscures hidden dependencies on network topology, spectral gap, or function approximation error; (iii) the tightness and novelty of the proof techniques relative to the concurrent Chen et al. (2021) discounted-reward analysis, since the authors' main selling point is the added difficulty of the average-reward case; and (iv) the absence, in the introduction, of any empirical validation or discussion of whether experiments are provided. Overall, the paper appears to make a solid theoretical contribution, but the writing should more precisely state the convergence metric, assumptions, and the technical innovations that distinguish it from closely related concurrent work.

comments.title: Sample complexity improvement factor claim
comments.quote: with sample complexity being O(ǫ[−][3] log[2] (ǫ[−][1] )). By applying mini-batch sampling update, we are able to improve the sample complexity by a factor of O(ǫ[−][1] ).
comments.explanation: The authors state their O(ε^{-2} log(ε^{-1})) improves upon Qiu et al.'s O(ε^{-3} log^2(ε^{-1})) 'by a factor of O(ε^{-1}).' Checking: the ratio is ε^{-3} log^2(ε^{-1}) / (ε^{-2} log(ε^{-1})) = ε^{-1} log(ε^{-1}), so the improvement factor is actually O(ε^{-1} log(ε^{-1})), not O(ε^{-1}).

comments.title: Assumption 3 contradicts practical example for η
comments.explanation: The example A = (1/deg(G))(deg(G)·I − L) gives diagonal entries A_ii = 1 − deg_i(G)/deg(G), which equals 0 when vertex i has the maximum degree. Thus η = 0 in that case, contradicting the claimed η ≥ 1/deg(G). The standard Metropolis or lazy-Laplacian construction would give a positive lower bound, but the specific formula given here does not.

comments.title: Assumption 4: 'w = 0' likely should be 'w ≠ 0'
comments.quote: the inequality w[T] Aπθ w < 0 holds for any w = 0
comments.explanation: As written, the claim is that w^T A_{π_θ} w < 0 when w = 0, which is trivially false since the quadratic form is 0 at w = 0. The intended statement is clearly 'for any w ≠ 0' (i.e., A_{π_θ} is negative definite on nonzero vectors).

comments.title: Algorithm 1 input lists K but loop uses Kc
comments.quote: step-size β, critic step iteration number K, critic batch size M
comments.explanation: The input declares the iteration count as K, but the loop bound uses Kc and the output is wKc. This is a minor inconsistency in naming — almost certainly 'K' in the input line should be 'Kc'.

comments.title: Critic step uses µ_{k,τ} but only µ_{k,τ+1} is updated; initial condition unspecified
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1 line 7, µ^i_{k,τ+1} is computed from µ^i_{k,τ} and r^i_{k,τ+1}. Then line 8 uses µ^i_{k,τ} (not the just-updated value) in the TD-error. The initial condition for µ^i_{k,0} across iterations is not explicitly specified — particularly whether µ carries over between outer k iterations or resets. This could affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Consensus update indexing inconsistency
comments.quote: Consensus Update wk[i] +1[←][] j∈Ni[A][(][i, j][)][ ·][w][˜] k[j]
comments.explanation: The consensus update sums over j ∈ N_i. Under Assumption 3, A(i,j) = 0 for non-neighbors and A(i,i) ≥ η > 0. For the doubly stochastic property to yield a proper convex combination, the sum should include j = i as well. The notation N_i may or may not include i itself; this is not specified.

comments.title: Step-size constraint involves η^{N-1} which can be extremely small
comments.explanation: The first term in the step-size bound comes from a spectral-gap-like quantity. Since Assumption 3 guarantees only η ≥ 1/N, η^{N-1} can be as small as (1/N)^{N-1}, making this constraint extraordinarily restrictive in practice for large N. The O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)) headline complexity hides this dependence because it 'treats N as a fixed constant'. The practical dependence on N through these step-size constraints is much worse than the stated N² scaling suggests.

comments.title: Use of 'unbiased' for global TD-error conflates conditional expectation with advantage
comments.quote: E[δt|st, at] = Aθ(st, at). Thus, we can use this global TD-error as an unbiased estimate of the advantage function.
comments.explanation: The standard identity E[δ_t | s_t, a_t] = A_θ(s_t, a_t) holds only when V_θ is the true value function and μ_t equals J(θ). In the algorithm V_θ is approximated by φ(s)^T w and μ_t is a running estimate of J(θ), so δ_t is biased in general. Calling δ_t an unbiased estimate is misleading without this caveat.

comments.title: Inconsistency between actor step-size notation in critic average-reward update vs. actor step
comments.quote: where µt+1 = (1 − α) · µt + α · ¯rt+1 is the estimate for the average long term return, and α is the step-size for the actor step.
comments.explanation: Earlier, the average-reward estimate in the critic uses step-size β: µ^i_{k,τ+1} = (1−β)µ^i_{k,τ} + β·r^i_{k,τ+1}. Here in the actor section, the text writes µt+1 = (1−α)µt + α·r̄_{t+1} and calls α the actor step-size. It is confusing whether the running average of rewards used in computing the actor's TD-error is being updated with the actor step-size α (unusual since µ is a critic-side estimate) or whether this is a notational slip.

comments.title: Communication complexity expression drops N dependence inconsistently
comments.quote: And the communication complexity is (Kc + tgossip)T= O(1 ǫ[log(][N] ǫ[))
comments.explanation: The communication complexity is written as O((1/ε) log(N/ε)). The bound t_gossip ≥ (1/2) log_{(1−η^{N−1})^{−1}}(64κ_3 N^3/ε) depends on N through both N^3 and η^{N−1} in the base of the log. For small η^{N−1}, log((1−η^{N−1})^{-1}) ≈ η^{N−1}, so t_gossip could scale like η^{-(N-1)}. This is a potentially serious hidden dependence not reflected in the stated bound.

comments.title: Possible missing factor of 1/ε in bits-of-information complexity
comments.quote: The amount of information (in terms of bits) is (KcNK + BNtgossip)T = O( ǫ[N][2][log][N] ǫ[)
comments.explanation: Checking the computation: K_c·N·K·T = O(log(N/ε))·N·K·O(1/ε) = O(NK/ε · log(N/ε)), and B·N·t_gossip·T = O(N/ε)·N·O(log(N/ε))·O(1/ε) = O(N²/ε² · log(N/ε)). The dominant term is O(N²/ε² · log(N/ε)), not O(N²/ε · log(N/ε)) as stated. There appears to be a missing factor of 1/ε. Also, reusing K for both feature dimension and 'bits per weight' is confusing.

comments.title: Theorem 3 references 'same setting as in Theorem 1' but should refer to Theorem 2
comments.quote: Suppose for the same setting as in Theorem 1 holds so that E[[][N] i=1[||][w] K[i] c[−][w] θ[∗][||][2][]][≤] 288ǫ N
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 is the Policy Gradient Theorem for MARL, a structural result with no 'setting' pertaining to the critic's MSE bound on ||w^i_{K_c} − w*_θ||². The critic convergence and sample complexity bound is Theorem 2. This is a reference mistake that would confuse the reader.

comments.title: Batch size bound B contains unexplained parameters ρ and κ
comments.explanation: The bound on the actor batch size B introduces constants ρ and κ that are not defined in the passage. While they are likely mixing-time / ergodicity constants, the passage does not point to where these are defined. For a self-contained reading of the main theorem statement, this is underspecified.

comments.title: Comparison in Table 1 mixes different problem settings without caveat
comments.quote: |Thispaper|MAAR|Markovian|Markovian|O(N 2/ǫ2 log(N/ǫ))|
comments.explanation: Table 1 lists this paper's complexity as O(N^2/ε^2 log(N/ε)) while other entries (Xu et al. 2020, Chen et al. 2021) are listed as O(ε^{-2} log(ε^{-1})) without N-dependence shown. Chen et al. (2021) is MADR — their complexity presumably also has hidden N-dependence that has been suppressed. Presenting only this paper's N-dependence explicitly while hiding it for the comparison row could mislead a reader into thinking this paper has worse N-scaling than Chen et al.

comments.title: Step-size constraint involves η^{N-1} which can be extremely small
comments.explanation: The first term in the step-size bound appears to be (1-η^{N-1})/(2η^{N-1}) or similar, coming from a spectral-gap-like quantity. Since Assumption 3 guarantees only η ≥ 1/N (from the practical choice A = I - L/deg(G)), η^{N-1} can be as small as (1/N)^{N-1}, making this constraint extraordinarily restrictive in practice for large N. The O(N²/ε² log(N/ε)) headline complexity hides this dependence because it 'treats N as a fixed constant'. The authors acknowledge this caveat ('treating network size N as a fixed constant') but the practical dependence on N through these step-size constraints is much worse than the stated N² scaling suggests. This is a framing/overclaim concern more than an error.

comments.title: Consensus update uses ~w^i instead of ~w^j
comments.explanation: The prose says 'agent i will further update the approximation parameter w^i through a weighted average of its local and neighboring agents' parameters'. The formula shown sums A(i,j) times ~w_k^i (with superscript i, not j). This contradicts the consensus update in the paper summary (w^i_{k+1} = Σ_{j∈N_i} A(i,j) · ~w^j_k) and Algorithm 1 line 11 which correctly shows ~w_k^j. This is a plausible OCR issue, but if taken literally the expression sums A(i,j)·~w^i over neighbors which makes no sense as a consensus update. Likely an OCR error rather than an author error, but the inline text has the inconsistency.

comments.title: Score function written with (s_{t,l}, a_{t,l}^i) instead of conditional form
comments.quote: ψt,l[i][=][∇][θ][i][ log][ π] θ[i] t[i][(][s][t,l][, a] t,l[i][)][ is the local score function for agent][ i]
comments.explanation: The paper defines the score function earlier as ψ_θ(s,a) = ∇_θ log π_θ(a|s), i.e., the log of a conditional probability. Here it is written as log π_{θ_t^i}^i(s_{t,l}, a_{t,l}^i) with a comma between state and action, suggesting a joint rather than conditional. This is almost certainly a notational shorthand or OCR artifact; any reader familiar with policy gradient would read it as log π(a|s). Minor ambiguity only.

comments.title: Use of 'unbiased' for global TD-error conflates conditional expectation with advantage
comments.quote: E[δt|st, at] = Aθ(st, at). Thus, we can use this global TD-error as an unbiased estimate of the advantage function.
comments.explanation: The standard identity E[δ_t | s_t, a_t] = A_θ(s_t, a_t) holds only when V_θ is the true value function and μ_t equals J(θ). In the algorithm V_θ is approximated by φ(s)^T w and μ_t is a running estimate of J(θ), so δ_t is biased in general. The 'unbiased estimate' claim thus relies on idealized quantities, not the actual algorithm iterates. The authors presumably handle this bias in the formal analysis (as mentioned later: 'handle bias error in the critic step'). For a methodology overview this is fine, but calling δ_t an unbiased estimate is misleading without this caveat.

comments.title: Inconsistency between actor step-size notation in critic average-reward update vs. actor step
comments.quote: where µt+1 = (1 − α) · µt + α · ¯rt+1 is the estimate for the average long term return, and α is the step-size for the actor step.
comments.explanation: Earlier in the paper, the average-reward estimate in the critic (Algorithm 1) uses step-size β: µ^i_{k,τ+1} = (1−β)µ^i_{k,τ} + β·r^i_{k,τ+1}. Here in the actor section, the text writes µt+1 = (1−α)µt + α·r̄_{t+1} and calls α the actor step-size. It is confusing whether the running average of rewards used in computing the actor's TD-error is being updated with the actor step-size α (which would be unusual since µ is a critic-side estimate) or whether this is a notational slip. The summary's 'Local actor TD-error' uses µ_t without specifying its update rule in the actor step. A reader could reasonably be misled into thinking two different step-sizes govern the same running average, or that the actor uses a separate µ updated with α. This needs clarification.

comments.title: Communication complexity expression drops N dependence inconsistently
comments.quote: And the communication complexity is (Kc + tgossip)T= O(1 ǫ[log(][N] ǫ[))
comments.explanation: The communication complexity is written as O((1/ε) log(N/ε)), but in the abstract/intro it was stated as O(ε^{-1} log(ε^{-1})) without N. Checking: T = O(1/ε), K_c = O(log(N/ε)), t_gossip = O(log(N/ε) · something involving N since the bound t_gossip ≥ (1/2) log_{(1−η^{N−1})^{−1}}(64κ_3 N^3/ε) depends on N through both N^3 and η^{N−1} in the base of the log. The O(1/ε · log(N/ε)) claim seems to hide the N-dependence in the base (1−η^{N−1})^{−1}, which for small η^{N−1} makes the effective log factor scale like N (since log((1−η^{N−1})^{-1}) ≈ η^{N−1} for small η^{N−1}, so t_gossip could scale like η^{-(N-1)}). This is a potentially serious hidden dependence that is not reflected in the communication complexity bound as stated.

comments.title: Unclear quantity 'K' in bits-of-information complexity expression
comments.quote: The amount of information (in terms of bits) is (KcNK + BNtgossip)T = O( ǫ[N][2][log][N] ǫ[)
comments.explanation: The factor K here presumably refers to the feature dimension K (from φ(s) ∈ R^K), used as a proxy for bits to transmit a weight vector. But in the actor step, only scalar TD-errors are transmitted, so BN·t_gossip reflects scalar transmissions correctly. For the critic, K_c·N·K reflects K-dimensional weight vectors transmitted per consensus round. This reuse of K for 'bits per weight' as well as feature dimension is somewhat confusing — plus calling it 'bits' when K is really dimensionality (each entry being a real number) is imprecise. Finally, the stated total O(N²/ε · log(N/ε)) should be checked: K_c·N·K·T = O(log(N/ε))·N·K·O(1/ε) = O(NK/ε · log(N/ε)), and B·N·t_gossip·T = O(N/ε)·N·O(log(N/ε))·O(1/ε) = O(N²/ε² · log(N/ε)). The dominant term is O(N²/ε² · log(N/ε)), not O(N²/ε · log(N/ε)) as stated. There appears to be a missing factor of 1/ε.

comments.title: Theorem 3 references 'same setting as in Theorem 1' but should likely refer to Theorem 2
comments.quote: Suppose for the same setting as in Theorem 1 holds so that E[[][N] i=1[||][w] K[i] c[−][w] θ[∗][||][2][]][≤] 288ǫ N
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 is the Policy Gradient Theorem for MARL, which is a structural result and has no 'setting' pertaining to the critic's MSE bound on ||w^i_{K_c} − w*_θ||². The critic convergence and sample complexity bound is Theorem 2. This is almost certainly a typo or OCR-related confusion; but as written it would confuse the reader, since Theorem 1 does not establish the stated bound. Given the OCR note at the top, I'd normally be lenient, but here 'Theorem 1' vs 'Theorem 2' is not an OCR artifact — it's a reference mistake that a careful reader would flag.

comments.title: Batch size bound B contains unexplained parameters ρ and κ
comments.explanation: The bound on the actor batch size B introduces constants ρ and κ (and possibly κ_ρ) that are not defined in the passage or summary up to this point. While they are likely mixing-time / ergodicity constants standard in Markovian sampling analysis (e.g., from Levin-Peres), the passage does not point to where these are defined. For a self-contained reading of the main theorem statement, this is underspecified. Readers reproducing the bound would need to hunt for definitions elsewhere.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely theoretical gap—finite-time/sample-complexity guarantees for fully decentralized cooperative MARL under the average-reward criterion and without joint-action knowledge—and the stated contribution appears potentially significant, especially if the claimed \(O(\epsilon^{-2}\log(1/\epsilon))\) sample complexity and reduced communication requirements are rigorously established under reasonable assumptions. The introduction is generally well motivated and clearly positions the work relative to discounted-reward MARL, centralized-critic methods, and policy-evaluation-only analyses; however, the presentation would benefit from sharper precision in several places. In particular, the claims of “first-ever” results and matching single-agent complexity should be carefully qualified by assumptions such as mixing conditions, function approximation class, network connectivity, dependence on \(N\), batch sizes, and whether convergence is to a stationary point, a neighborhood, or an approximate Nash/team optimum. The sample-complexity statements are somewhat inconsistent in the excerpt—e.g., the abstract gives \(O(N^2/\epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))\), while the contribution section sometimes treats \(N\) as fixed and reports \(O(\epsilon^{-2}\log(\epsilon^{-1}))\)—so the paper should make the dependence on network size, communication rounds, and constants explicit and easy to compare against prior work. Clarity is also weakened by formatting/typographical artifacts and by some vague phrases such as “closeness to the neighborhood of stationary,” which should be formalized early. Overall, the paper seems technically ambitious and potentially valuable, but its impact will depend heavily on the strength and transparency of the assumptions, the exact decentralization/information model, and whether the theoretical comparison to single-agent and existing MARL methods remains fair once all dependencies and communication costs are included.

comments.title: Sample-complexity comparisons need a fixed-network-size or ε-only qualifier
comments.quote: We show that the sample complexity of this algorithm is O(N[2] /ϵ[2] log(N/ϵ)). Interestingly, this sample complexity bound matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent actorcritic algorithms for reinforcement learning. ... achieved a sample complexity of O(ϵ[−][2] log(ϵ[−][1] )), which is the same as ours. ... The sample complexity of O(ϵ[−][2] log(ϵ[−][1] )) matches the state-of-the-art single-agent actor-critic RL by Xu et al. (2020) and the discounted MARL by Chen et al. (2021).
comments.explanation: Several passages compare the paper’s sample complexity to single-agent or discounted MARL actor-critic bounds as though it were simply O(ε^{-2} log(ε^{-1})). However, the paper’s own displayed bound includes explicit network-size dependence, apparently O(N^2 ε^{-2} log(N/ε)). The intended comparison is likely that the ε-dependence matches prior work when N is treated as fixed, and some later text partially supports that interpretation. But the abstract, related-work comparison, and theorem discussion omit that qualifier in places, which can be read as claiming equality of the full complexity including N-scaling. The comparison should be stated as matching in ε-dependence, or as holding for fixed N.

comments.title: Possible ambiguity about lacking joint state information despite assuming global state observability
comments.quote: In MARL, the decentralized nature and the lack of joint action-state information further complicate the algorithm design and analysis in the average reward setting.
comments.explanation: The formal model later assumes all agents observe the current global state, while each agent observes only its own action and reward. The authors likely mean that agents lack the full joint state-action pair because the joint action component is unavailable. Still, the phrase “lack of joint action-state information” could be read as saying agents also lack global state information, which would contradict the stated model. This is a wording issue, but it could mislead readers about the information structure being analyzed.

comments.title: Communication-efficiency comparisons are underqualified
comments.quote: By contrast, the state-of-the-art MARL requires a communication round per sampling. ... Zhang et al. (2018) needed a communication round after each sampling. By contrast, in this paper, we only need a communication round per O(ϵ[−][1] ) sampling.
comments.explanation: The batching idea does support a meaningful reduction in communication rounds compared with methods that communicate after every sample. However, the broad claim that “the state-of-the-art MARL” requires communication per sample is underqualified, especially since the paper itself discusses related mini-batch actor-critic work in other MARL settings. Similarly, saying the method needs one communication round per O(ε^{-1}) samples is best interpreted as an asymptotic total-samples-to-total-communication ratio under the theorem’s parameter choices. The actual communication cost also includes critic consensus, TD-error gossip, network dependence, and constants such as K_c and t_gossip. The comparison would be clearer if restricted to the relevant prior fully decentralized average-reward actor-critic class and phrased as an asymptotic ratio rather than a direct operational per-sample statement.

comments.title: Overbroad claim of first finite-time convergence results for MARL
comments.quote: We note that, together with our work, these are the first finite-time convergence results for MARL.
comments.explanation: The authors likely intend this to refer to finite-time convergence results for multi-agent actor-critic methods with policy improvement. In that narrower sense, the surrounding comparison to Chen et al. (2021) may be plausible. But the sentence says “for MARL” broadly, which is difficult to reconcile with the immediately preceding discussion of Doan et al. (2019), described by the authors themselves as giving a sample-complexity result for decentralized MARL TD(0) policy evaluation. Policy evaluation is not actor-critic policy improvement, but it is still part of MARL theory. A more accurate statement would restrict the claim to MARL actor-critic algorithms or to the specific fully decentralized average-reward setting studied here.

comments.title: Non-strict policy positivity is inconsistent with the later score-function assumption
comments.quote: Assumption 1. For any i ∈N , s ∈S, a[i] ∈A[i] and θ[i] ∈ R[m][i] , the policy function πθ[i][i][(][a][i][|][s][)][≥][0][.]
comments.explanation: The prose refers to policy “positivity,” and the later softmax examples are strictly positive. But the formal assumption only requires nonnegativity. That is not enough for the later score-function definition ψθ(s, a) = ∇θ log πθ(a|s) for every state-action pair, since log πθ(a|s) is undefined when πθ(a|s)=0. Standard likelihood-ratio policy-gradient arguments also require either full support or careful support restrictions and boundary handling. The assumption likely should require πθ^i(a^i|s)>0, or another later assumption should explicitly imply full support.

comments.title: Suggested consensus matrix appears not to satisfy the stated positive-diagonal condition
comments.quote: We remark that for a practical choice of A, one can use the following form A = ... where deg(G) is the degree of the graph G ... and L is the Laplacian matrix of the graph. It is easy to verify that this matrix is symmetric, doubly stochastic and η ≥ deg1(G)[≥] N[1][.]
comments.explanation: The intended construction appears to be the standard Laplacian consensus matrix, such as A = I - L/deg(G). If deg(G) is the maximum vertex degree, then any node with degree exactly deg(G) has diagonal entry A_ii = 1 - deg_i/deg(G) = 0. This violates the preceding assumption that A_ii ≥ η for some η>0, and contradicts the claimed lower bound η ≥ 1/deg(G). A lazy version such as A = I - L/(deg(G)+1) would give positive diagonals. The issue may be an OCR or typesetting omission, but as written the verification claim is false.

comments.title: Uniform-in-θ smoothness and approximation-error claims are not justified by the stated assumptions
comments.quote: We note that Assumption 6 can be satisfied by the class of soft-max policy under the Assumption 1, as in (Guo et al., 2021). ... Define the approximation error introduced by using linear approximation in the critic step, ξapprox[critic] = maxθ∈R... E_s∼dθ[|Vπθ(s) − V*wπθ(s)|2].
comments.explanation: Two later uniform claims rely on control over the entire, apparently unbounded, policy-parameter space: the global Lipschitz-gradient assumption for J(θ), and the finiteness of the maximum critic approximation error over all θ. Assumption 1 gives irreducibility and aperiodicity for each fixed θ, but it does not impose compactness, uniformly positive action probabilities, or a uniform mixing/spectral-gap condition. For softmax policies with unbounded parameters, action probabilities can approach zero and the induced chain can approach reducibility while remaining irreducible for every finite θ. In average-reward problems, derivatives of the stationary distribution, the bias function, and the projection error can then fail to be uniformly bounded. These claims would be clearer if they were stated as additional assumptions, restricted to a compact parameter set, or derived from a uniform ergodicity condition.

comments.title: Algorithm 1’s loop structure is ambiguous about the joint action and single environment transition
comments.quote: 3 for all i ∈N do 4 for τ = 0, · · · , M − 1 do 5 Execute action a[i] k,τ[∼][π] θ[i] t[i][(][·|][s][k,τ][)][;] 6 Observe the state sk,τ +1 and reward rk,τ[i] +1[;]
comments.explanation: The model says the environment transition depends on the joint action a=(a^1,...,a^N). The intended sampling procedure is presumably that, for each τ, all agents simultaneously draw local actions, the joint action is applied once, one common next state is generated, and each agent observes its local reward. But the pseudocode nests the τ-loop inside “for all i,” which could be read as each agent independently executing M environment transitions, or as the environment transitioning separately for each local action. The algorithm would be clearer if the sampling loop were organized by τ first, with all agents acting in parallel before a single transition.

comments.title: Consensus update in prose uses the wrong neighbor index
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 appears to use the correct consensus update, where agent i averages tentative critic parameters received from neighbors: w_{k+1}^i = ∑_{j∈N_i} A(i,j) \tilde w_k^j. The prose equation, however, has \tilde w_k^i inside the summand. Read literally, the sum over j would only multiply agent i’s own tentative parameter by the row sum, so no neighbor information would enter. This is likely a typo, but it is technically inconsistent and could mislead an implementation based on the text.

comments.title: TD-error unbiasedness claims ignore average-reward estimation and critic approximation
comments.quote: δt = rt+1 − μt + Vθ(st+1) − Vθ(st), where μt+1 = (1 − α) · μt + α · ¯rt+1 is the estimate for the average long term return ... Hence, we have that the expected global TD-error is the advantage function, i.e., E[δt|st, at] = Aθ(st, at). ... For any time t, the average of the local TD-errors is an unbiased estimate of the advantage function.
comments.explanation: The exact identity E[δ_t | s_t,a_t] = A_θ(s_t,a_t) is standard when the TD error uses the true average reward J(θ) and the true differential value V_θ. The displayed algorithmic quantities instead use a running estimate μ_t, and later each agent uses an approximate local critic φ(s)^T w_t^i. With constant-step recursions, μ_t is generally transient, random, and correlated with the trajectory; replacing J(θ) by μ_t does not preserve exact unbiasedness. Likewise, averaging local TD errors formed with different approximate critics gives an approximate value-difference term, not necessarily the true differential value difference. The statements would be correct for an idealized exact-value/exact-average-reward case, but as written they overstate what the actual algorithmic estimators guarantee.

comments.title: Batch TD-error vector indexing appears inconsistent with the samples used in the actor update
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 constructs a batch of TD errors indexed by l=0,...,B−1, and the actor update sums over l=0 to B−1. The matrix Δ_0 also appears to contain rows for δ_{t,0}^i through δ_{t,B−1}^i. The quoted assignment labels the resulting vector as \tilde δ_{t,1:B−1}^i, which would omit the l=0 entry and contain only B−1 elements. This is probably an indexing typo or OCR issue, but as written it creates an off-by-one ambiguity.

comments.title: Stationarity guarantee is ambiguous about which iterate is bounded
comments.quote: then we have E[||∇θJ(θt)||[2] ] ≤ ϵ + O(ξapprox[critic][)]
comments.explanation: The intended conclusion is presumably the standard nonconvex finite-time guarantee: either the average over iterates is small, the best iterate is small, or a uniformly sampled output iterate has small expected squared gradient norm. As written, the theorem states E||∇J(θ_t)||² ≤ ε without specifying whether t is arbitrary, the final iterate, the best iterate, or a randomly selected iterate. A last-iterate guarantee for constant-step stochastic actor-critic would be substantially stronger and would require additional argument. The theorem should specify the output iterate or the averaging/minimum statement being proved.

comments.title: Communication “bits” calculation counts messages or scalars, not actual bits
comments.quote: Here, the overall communication cost is measured by the number of communication rounds rather than the size of bits transmitted over the network. ... The amount of information (in terms of bits) is (KcNK + BNtgossip)T = O( ϵ[N][2][log][N] ϵ[)][.]
comments.explanation: The expression (K_c N K + B N t_gossip)T appears to count transmitted real-valued coordinates or scalar messages: critic parameters of dimension K and scalar TD-error/gossip quantities. It is not a number of bits unless a quantization scheme, numerical precision, or finite-word model is specified. Counting communication rounds or transmitted scalars is a common and useful convention, but then the quantity should not be described as “bits.” The statement would be accurate if phrased as scalar transmissions or communicated real numbers.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely theoretical gap—finite-time/sample-complexity guarantees for fully decentralized cooperative MARL under the average-reward criterion and without joint-action knowledge—and the stated contribution appears potentially significant, especially if the claimed \(O(\epsilon^{-2}\log(1/\epsilon))\) sample complexity and reduced communication requirements are rigorously established under reasonable assumptions. The introduction is generally well motivated and clearly positions the work relative to discounted-reward MARL, centralized-critic methods, and policy-evaluation-only analyses; however, the presentation would benefit from sharper precision in several places. In particular, the claims of “first-ever” results and matching single-agent complexity should be carefully qualified by assumptions such as mixing conditions, function approximation class, network connectivity, dependence on \(N\), batch sizes, and whether convergence is to a stationary point, a neighborhood, or an approximate Nash/team optimum. The sample-complexity statements are somewhat inconsistent in the excerpt—e.g., the abstract gives \(O(N^2/\epsilon^2 \log(N/\epsilon))\), while the contribution section sometimes treats \(N\) as fixed and reports \(O(\epsilon^{-2}\log(\epsilon^{-1}))\)—so the paper should make the dependence on network size, communication rounds, and constants explicit and easy to compare against prior work. Clarity is also weakened by formatting/typographical artifacts and by some vague phrases such as “closeness to the neighborhood of stationary,” which should be formalized early. Overall, the paper seems technically ambitious and potentially valuable, but its impact will depend heavily on the strength and transparency of the assumptions, the exact decentralization/information model, and whether the theoretical comparison to single-agent and existing MARL methods remains fair once all dependencies and communication costs are included.

comments.title: Sample-complexity comparison to single-agent AC omits the fixed-N qualifier
comments.quote: We show that the sample complexity of this algorithm is O(N[2] /ǫ[2] log(N/ǫ)). Interestingly, this sample complexity bound matches that of the state-of-the-art single-agent actorcritic algorithms for reinforcement learning.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended comparison: later in the introduction, and in the related-work table, the authors appear to compare the dependence on the accuracy parameter ǫ, treating the network size N as fixed. Under that convention, O(N^2/ǫ^2 log(N/ǫ)) has the same ǫ-order as the single-agent O(ǫ^{-2} log(ǫ^{-1})) bounds. However, as written in the abstract, the statement says the sample-complexity bound itself “matches” the single-agent bound immediately after displaying an explicit N^2 factor and log(N/ǫ) term. For readers interested in scalability with the number of agents, this is not a match unless N is regarded as a constant or unless the comparison is explicitly only in ǫ-dependence. The later bullet partially resolves this by saying “treating network size N as a fixed constant,” but the abstract claim remains overstated without that qualifier.

comments.title: Possible ambiguity about lacking joint state information despite assuming global state observability
comments.quote: In MARL, the decentralized nature and the lack of joint action-state information further complicate the algorithm design and analysis in the average reward setting.
comments.explanation: The surrounding discussion consistently emphasizes that the paper removes the assumption of joint action knowledge, while still allowing all agents to observe the global state. The formal model later confirms this: all agents observe the current global state s_t, but each agent observes only its own action and reward. With that context, I think the authors likely mean that agents lack the full state-action pair only because the joint action component is unavailable. Still, the phrase “lack of joint action-state information” could reasonably be read as saying that agents also lack joint/global state information, which would contradict the stated model. This is not a mathematical error, but it could mislead readers about the information structure being analyzed.

comments.title: Broad communication-complexity comparison is underqualified
comments.quote: By contrast, the state-of-the-art MARL requires a communication round per sampling.
comments.explanation: I see the intended point: the proposed mini-batch design separates sampling from consensus communication, whereas some classical decentralized actor-critic or TD-sharing schemes communicate after every sample. This is a meaningful contribution. However, the phrase “the state-of-the-art MARL” is quite broad, especially because the related-work section itself notes concurrent mini-batch Markovian actor-critic work for discounted MARL. Without specifying the comparison class—e.g., prior fully decentralized average-reward MARL actor-critic methods without joint action information—the statement reads as a general claim about all state-of-the-art MARL algorithms. That broader claim is not established in the passage and may not hold across different MARL settings or batching protocols.

comments.title: “Same as ours” omits the paper’s stated network-size dependence
comments.quote: They have applied batch sampling for both actor and critic steps and achieved a sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )), which is the same as ours.
comments.explanation: I read this as a comparison to Chen et al. (2021), and the intended point seems to be that the dependence on the accuracy parameter matches the state of the art. That interpretation is partly supported elsewhere in the paper, where the authors explicitly say that, treating the network size N as fixed, their complexity is O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1])). However, in the abstract and in Table 1 immediately below, this paper’s stated sample complexity is O(N 2/ǫ2 log(N/ǫ)), not simply O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1])). Thus, without the qualifier “for fixed N” or “in terms of ǫ,” the sentence can be read as claiming equality of the full sample-complexity scaling, including network-size dependence. That is not what the displayed bound for this paper says. This is fixable by qualifying the comparison as order-wise in ǫ or under fixed N.

comments.title: Overbroad claim of first finite-time convergence results for MARL
comments.quote: We note that, together with our work, these are the first finite-time convergence results for MARL.
comments.explanation: The authors likely intend this to refer to finite-time convergence results for multi-agent actor-critic methods with policy improvement, and in that narrower sense the surrounding discussion of Chen et al. (2021) makes the intended novelty plausible. However, the sentence as written says “for MARL” broadly. That is difficult to reconcile with the immediately preceding discussion of Doan et al. (2019), which the authors themselves describe as giving a sample complexity result for a decentralized MARL TD(0) policy-evaluation algorithm. Policy evaluation is not actor-critic policy improvement, but it is still commonly part of MARL theory. The broader phrasing therefore overstates the novelty and could mislead readers into thinking no finite-time MARL results existed at all. A more accurate formulation would be “among the first finite-time convergence results for MARL actor-critic algorithms” or “the first for the fully decentralized average-reward actor-critic setting considered here.”

comments.title: Non-strict policy positivity is inconsistent with the later score-function assumption
comments.quote: Assumption 1. For any i ∈N , s ∈S, a[i] ∈A[i] and θ[i] ∈ R[m][i] , the policy function πθ[i][i][(][a][i][|][s][)][≥][0][.]
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent here as requiring admissible stochastic policies and enough regularity to define the stationary distribution under each policy. The surrounding prose even refers to “positivity,” and later the paper uses softmax policies, which are strictly positive. However, the formal condition shown here only gives nonnegativity. That is not enough for the later definition of the score function, “ψθ(s, a) = ∇θ log πθ(a|s),” for every state-action pair, because log πθ(a|s) is undefined when πθ(a|s)=0. It is also not enough for standard likelihood-ratio policy-gradient arguments unless one either assumes full support or carefully restricts to the support and handles boundary terms. This may be a simple symbol-level issue—the authors likely intended πθ^i(a^i|s)>0, or Assumption 6 implicitly enforces positivity—but as written the assumptions are formally inconsistent.

comments.title: Suggested consensus matrix appears not to satisfy the stated positive-diagonal condition
comments.quote: We remark that for a practical choice of A, one can use the following form A = 1[−][L][)][,][where][deg][(][G][)][is][the][degree][of][the][graph][G][(i.e.][the][maximal][vertex][de-] deg(G)[(][deg][(][G][)][ ·][ I] gree), I is the identity matrix of conforming dimensionality, and L is the Laplacian matrix of the graph. It is easy to verify that this matrix is symmetric, doubly stochastic and η ≥ deg1(G)[≥] N[1][.]
comments.explanation: The intended construction seems to be the common Laplacian-based consensus matrix, probably something like A = I - L/deg(G) or A = (deg(G)I-L)/deg(G). Such a matrix is symmetric and row/column stochastic for an undirected graph. But if deg(G) is the maximum vertex degree, then any node with degree exactly deg(G) has diagonal entry Aii = 1 - deg_i/deg(G) = 0. This violates the immediately preceding Assumption 3 requirement that Aii ≥ η for some η>0. It also contradicts the claimed lower bound η ≥ 1/deg(G). A standard fix would be to use a lazy version such as A = I - L/(deg(G)+1), which gives positive diagonals and off-diagonal weights at least 1/(deg(G)+1). It is possible that the OCR dropped a “+1” in the denominator, but if the displayed construction is indeed as written, the verification claim is false.

comments.title: Softmax does not by itself seem to justify the global Lipschitz-gradient assumption under only Assumption 1
comments.quote: We note that Assumption 6 can be satisfied by the class of soft-max policy under the Assumption 1, as in (Guo et al., 2021).
comments.explanation: I see why the authors make this comment: with finite state/action spaces and bounded features, tabular or linearly parameterized softmax policies have bounded score functions, so part 1 of Assumption 6 is plausible. The more delicate part is the global Lipschitz property of ∇θJ(θ) in the average-reward setting. Assumption 1 requires irreducibility and aperiodicity for each θ, but it does not appear to impose a uniform mixing/spectral-gap condition over the entire, apparently unbounded, parameter space. For softmax policies, action probabilities can become arbitrarily close to zero as ||θ|| grows, and the induced Markov chain can become arbitrarily close to reducible even though it remains irreducible for each finite θ. In average-reward policy-gradient analysis, derivatives of the stationary distribution and bias function typically require uniform ergodicity-type control to obtain global Lipschitz constants. Thus the statement that softmax under Assumption 1 satisfies Assumption 6 seems stronger than what Assumption 1 alone guarantees. This may be true under additional bounded-parameter or uniform ergodicity conditions, but those are not stated here.

comments.title: Algorithm 1’s loop structure is ambiguous about the joint action and single environment transition
comments.quote: 3 for all i ∈N do 4 for τ = 0, · · · , M − 1 do 5 Execute action a[i] k,τ[∼][π] θ[i] t[i][(][·|][s][k,τ][)][;] 6 Observe the state sk,τ +1 and reward rk,τ[i] +1[;]
comments.explanation: The surrounding model is clear that the environment transition depends on the joint action a=(a^1,...,a^N), while each agent observes only its own action and reward. So I infer that, for each sample τ, all agents simultaneously draw their local actions, the joint action is applied once, the common next state sk,τ+1 is generated, and each agent observes its local reward. However, the pseudocode nests the τ-loop inside “for all i,” which can be read as each agent independently executing M transitions from the shared state sequence, or as the environment transitioning separately for each local action. That would contradict the networked MDP definition. A careful reader can probably reconstruct the intended parallel execution, but the algorithm would be more reproducible if the sampling loop were organized as: for each τ, all agents choose local actions in parallel, the environment transitions once according to P(·|sk,τ,ak,τ), and then each agent updates its local TD quantities.

comments.title: Consensus update in prose uses the wrong neighbor index
comments.explanation: The intended operation is clear from Algorithm 1: after each agent forms its tentative critic parameter \(\tilde w_k^j\), agent \(i\) should average the tentative parameters received from neighbors, i.e. something of the form \(w_{k+1}^i=\sum_{j\in\mathcal N_i}A(i,j)\tilde w_k^j\). If I read the prose equation literally, however, the summand contains \(\tilde w_k^i\), not \(\tilde w_k^j\). Then the sum over \(j\) would just multiply agent \(i\)'s own tentative parameter by the row sum of the weights, and no neighbor information would enter. The algorithm line immediately above appears to have the correct \(j\)-indexed version, so the method is recoverable from context, but the displayed/prose formula is technically inconsistent and could mislead a reader implementing from the text.

comments.title: The TD-error is called an unbiased advantage estimate despite using an estimated average reward and, later, approximate values
comments.quote: δt = rt+1 − µt + Vθ(st+1) − Vθ(st), where µt+1 = (1 − α) · µt + α · ¯rt+1 is the estimate for the average long term return, and α is the step-size for the actor step. Hence, we have that the expected global TD-error is the advantage function, i.e., E[δt|st, at] = Aθ(st, at).
comments.explanation: The basic identity the authors are invoking is standard in average-reward policy-gradient methods: if the TD error is formed with the true average reward \(J(\theta)\) and the true differential value \(V_\theta\), then \(\mathbb E[\bar r_{t+1}-J(\theta)+V_\theta(s_{t+1})-V_\theta(s_t)\mid s_t,a_t]=A_\theta(s_t,a_t)\). However, the expression in the passage uses \(\mu_t\), described as a running estimate of the average return, rather than \(J(\theta)\). With a constant-stepsize recursion, \(\mu_t\) is generally transient, random, and correlated with the trajectory; conditional on \((s_t,a_t)\), replacing \(J(\theta)\) by \(\mu_t\) does not preserve exact unbiasedness unless an additional condition such as \(\mu_t=J(\theta)\) is assumed. The later algorithm also uses \(\phi(s)^T w_t^i\) rather than the exact \(V_\theta\), introducing another approximation. The surrounding context suggests the authors may analyze these errors later, but the statement here is stronger than what the displayed recursion justifies.

comments.title: Average local TD-error is asserted to be unbiased without qualifying critic disagreement/approximation
comments.quote: For any time t, the average of the local TD-errors is an unbiased estimate of the advantage function.
comments.explanation: The intended intuition is reasonable: averaging \(r_{t+1}^i-\mu_t^i+V_\theta(s_{t+1})-V_\theta(s_t)\) over agents gives the global TD error when the same exact value function is used and \(\mu_t=N^{-1}\sum_i\mu_t^i\). But in the actual actor update in Algorithm 2, each agent forms \(\delta_{t,l}^i\) using its own critic parameter \(w_t^i\), so the averaged TD error contains \(N^{-1}\sum_i[\phi(s_{t+1})^T w_t^i-\phi(s_t)^T w_t^i]\), not necessarily the exact differential value difference. Moreover, as above, the \(\mu_t^i\)'s are running estimates rather than known expected rewards. Thus the average local TD error is at best an approximate or asymptotically controlled estimator of the advantage unless additional assumptions are imposed. The claim would be accurate if explicitly stated for the idealized case with exact \(J(\theta)\) and exact/common \(V_\theta\), but as written it overstates what the algorithmic quantities guarantee.

comments.title: Batch TD-error vector indexing appears inconsistent with the samples used in the actor update
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 constructs a batch of TD errors indexed by \(l=0,\ldots,B-1\), and the actor update on the next line sums \(l=0\) to \(B-1\). The matrix \(\Delta_0\) likewise appears to include rows for \(\delta_{t,0}^i\) through \(\delta_{t,B-1}^i\). The assignment quoted here, however, labels the resulting vector as \(\tilde\delta_{t,1:B-1}^i\), which would omit the \(l=0\) entry and contain only \(B-1\) elements. This is likely just an indexing typo or OCR-related loss of a leading 0, because the surrounding algorithm clearly needs all \(B\) estimates, but as written it creates a real off-by-one ambiguity in the pseudocode.

comments.title: Finite approximation-error term is not justified over an unbounded policy-parameter space
comments.quote: Define the approximation error introduced by using linear approximation in the critic step, ξapprox[critic][=] maxθ∈Ri∈N[mi][E][s][∼][d][θ][[][|][V][π][θ][(][s][)][ −][V][w] πθ[∗][(][s][)][|][2][]][.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: this is the standard irreducible approximation error from projecting the true differential value function onto the chosen linear feature class, and it is natural for the final stationarity bound to contain such a term. However, the definition takes a maximum over the entire unconstrained parameter space \(\mathbb R^{\sum_i m_i}\). From the assumptions shown earlier, each induced chain is irreducible and aperiodic for each fixed \(\theta\), but there is no explicit compactness of the parameter set, uniform lower bound on policy probabilities, or uniform mixing/geometric-ergodicity condition. In finite-state average-reward problems, the differential value function can grow without a uniform mixing bound as policies approach nearly reducible dynamics, even when rewards are bounded. Thus it is not clear that this maximum exists or is finite. Assumption 5 gives a uniform negative-definiteness condition for the projected TD matrix and helps bound the critic solution \(w_\theta^*\), but it does not by itself bound the true differential value \(V^{\pi_\theta}\) or the projection error uniformly over all \(\theta\). The result would be clearer if this were an explicit assumption, a supremum over a compact parameter set, or derived from a uniform ergodicity condition.

comments.title: Stationarity guarantee is ambiguous about which iterate is bounded
comments.quote: then we have E[||∇θJ(θt)||[2] ] ≤ ǫ + O(ξapprox[critic][)]
comments.explanation:
The intended conclusion is presumably the usual nonconvex actor-critic guarantee that some output iterate, or an iterate sampled uniformly from \(\{0,\ldots,T-1\}\), has small expected squared gradient norm. That would be consistent with standard finite-time analyses of stochastic nonconvex optimization, where one typically proves \( rac1T\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\mathbb E\|
abla J(	heta_t)\|^2\le \epsilon\) or \(\min_t \mathbb E\|
abla J(	heta_t)\|^2\le \epsilon\). As written, however, the theorem says \(\mathbb E\|
abla J(	heta_t)\|^2\le\epsilon\) without specifying whether \(t\) is arbitrary, the final iterate, the best iterate, or a randomly selected iterate. A last-iterate guarantee for constant-stepsize stochastic actor-critic would be substantially stronger and would require additional argument. The surrounding text does not resolve this ambiguity, especially because the algorithm description does not appear to define a random output iterate.

comments.title: Sample-complexity comparison suppresses the stated dependence on the number of agents
comments.quote: Theorem 3 concludes the overall sample complexity of our proposed actor-critic algorithm. The sample complexity of O(ǫ[−][2] log(ǫ[−][1] )) matches the state-of-the-art single-agent actor-critic RL by Xu et al. (2020) and the discounted MARL by Chen et al. (2021).
comments.explanation: The theorem statement just above gives a total complexity with explicit network-size dependence, rendered in the OCR as \((B+MK_c)T = O([N] ǫ[2][ 2][log(][N] ǫ[))]\), and the paper summary/table indicate this is intended to be \(O(N^2\epsilon^{-2}\log(N/\epsilon))\). The prose then states simply \(O(\epsilon^{-2}\log(\epsilon^{-1}))\) and says it matches single-agent actor-critic. I can see the authors’ intended comparison: they are emphasizing the dependence on the accuracy parameter \(\epsilon\), treating \(N\) as fixed, as they did elsewhere. But in this passage that qualifier is omitted immediately after a theorem with explicit \(N\)-dependence. A careful reader could incorrectly conclude that the full sample complexity, including scaling with the number of agents, matches the single-agent result. The statement should say that the \(\epsilon\)-dependence matches, or that the comparison treats \(N\) as a fixed constant.

comments.title: Communication ‘bits’ calculation counts messages/scalars, not actual bits
comments.quote: Here, the overall communication cost is measured by the number of communication rounds rather than the size of bits transmitted over the network. ... The amount of information (in terms of bits) is (KcNK + BNtgossip)T = O( ǫ[N][2][log][N] ǫ[)][.]
comments.explanation: The authors correctly distinguish communication rounds from the amount of information exchanged, which is an important distinction in decentralized MARL. The remaining concern is that the expression \((K_c N K + B N t_{gossip})T\) appears to count transmitted real-valued coordinates or scalar messages: critic parameters have dimension \(K\), and actor TD-sharing/gossip sends scalar TD errors or related quantities. It is not, by itself, a number of bits. Without a quantization scheme, numerical precision, or finite-word model, real-valued communication has unspecified—or in the idealized real-RAM model, effectively infinite—bit cost. This is a standard convention issue in distributed optimization: many papers count communication rounds or transmitted scalars, but then they should not describe that quantity as “bits” unless precision is specified. The statement is therefore misleading even if the asymptotic count of scalar transmissions is intended.

comments.title: Claim of better communication efficiency relies on asymptotic sampling-per-round comparison but omits constants and network dependence
comments.quote: Specifically, Zhang et al. (2018) needed a communication round after each sampling. By contrast, in this paper, we only need a communication round per O(ǫ[−][1] ) sampling.
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is reasonable: batching samples before communication can reduce the number of communication rounds relative to schemes that communicate after every sample. The theorem also supports a ratio of total samples to total communication rounds on the order of \(1/\epsilon\), if one focuses on \(\epsilon\)-dependence. What is less clear is the precise comparison once the communication required by gossiping, critic consensus, and network-size dependence is included. The theorem’s communication complexity involves both \(K_c\) and \(t_{gossip}\), and the later paragraph acknowledges additional scaling with \(t_{gossip}\), \(B\), and \(K_c\). Thus the phrase “a communication round per \(O(\epsilon^{-1})\) sampling” is a useful intuition but can be read as a direct per-sample operational statement, whereas the algorithm still performs multiple gossip/consensus rounds per actor/critic phase and the constants/network dependence may be substantial. This is not fatal, but the claim would be more accurate if stated as an asymptotic ratio of total samples to total communication rounds under the theorem’s parameter choices.
